data.title: CPI At Long Last: Inflation Eases In September, Clearing The Way For A Fed Rate Cut
data.description:
The September CPI report came in softer than expected. Headline CPI rose 0.31%, below the 0.4% consensus, bringing the year-over-year rate to 3.013%. The core rate increased 0.227% last month, under the 0.3% forecast, resulting in a similar 3.019% year-over-year rate.

Both headline and core inflation eased compared to August’s numbers (+0.38% and +0.35%, respectively). Stocks and bonds rallied after the sanguine consumer price data. The Fed is now a clear go for a rate cut next week.

Digging into the report, the 3-month annualized change in core CPI is now 3.64%, still well above the Fed’s 2% year-over-year headline PCE target. Owners’ equivalent rent rose just 0.137%, the smallest increase since November 2020. Meanwhile, supercore CPI came in at 0.352%, up from 0.33% in August, with the annual rate now at 3.176%, down from 3.211% previously.

Core goods CPI, key to the tariff narrative, increased by just 0.2%, with new cars up 0.2% and used vehicle prices falling 0.4%. Hot was apparel up 0.7% in September. Core services also posted a tame 0.2% monthly climb, aided by a 0.4% drop in vehicle insurance costs. Airfares supported services CPI, with that category rising 2.7%.

The Now-Dated September CPI Came in Lighter than Expected

Headline and Core CPI Tame

YoY CPI Inflation Holding Near 3%, Rate Cuts on Tap

3-Month Annualized Core CPI Barely Below 4%

US Inflation Using Real-Time Shelter Price Data Near 2%

Stocks and bonds rose after the sanguine September CPI report. The SPDR® S&P 500® ETF (NYSEARCA:SPY) printed fresh all-time highs in the premarket, while the yield on the benchmark 10-year Treasury note ticked down to 3.97%.

There was strength in small caps, and the VIX dipped under 17. Gold and Bitcoin caught a morning bid, too.

S&P 500 ETF Hits A New Record

Treasury Rates Dipped After The Cool Inflation Numbers

Gold’s Losses Mitigated Post-CPI

VIX Now Under 17

Looking ahead, it’s a busy week on the macro to wrap up what has so far been a bullish month of October. The big story will be two-fold.

First, Wednesday afternoon’s Fed decision (which will almost certainly be a cut) will offer fresh clues on just how dovish the FOMC will be in the current easing cycle. The Fed may also announce the end of its balance sheet runoff.

Second, the Trump-Xi meeting, slated for Thursday, will be crucial for the lingering trade war. As it stands, the market expects the government shutdown to last 45 days, so we may have to wait until November for a re-opening.

Fed Meeting Next Week

Government Shutdown Seen Lasting 45 Days

Big Earnings Week Ahead

The Bottom Line

September CPI was cooler than expected. Stocks and bonds rallied after the inflation numbers hit the tape, indicative of high prospects for more Fed rate cuts. This was dated data, though, and the government shutdown means limited macro information hitting the tape in the next (likely) three weeks.

data.title: Trump Cancels Trade Talks With Canada Even As U.S. Seeks Broad Alliance To Check China's Rare Earth Dominance
data.description:
Overview

The US dollar is trading higher against all the G10 currencies today, but it has not broken out of this week's ranges. The Dollar Index is rising for the fourth session this week and has recovered last week's 0.55% decline. The greenback is more mixed against emerging market currencies. While the JP Morgan Emerging Market Currency Index is up about 0.15% this week, the MSCI gauge is off by about 0.10%.

Equities are mostly firmer today. In the Asia Pacific region, the Nikkei and China's CSI 300 gained more than 1%, but South Korea's Kospi jumped 2.5% and led the region higher this week with a 5.1% gain. President Lee will meet President Trump on October 29 and President Xi on November 1. The South Korean won recovered from six-month lows following verbal intervention by the finance minister. Despite a better preliminary PMI, the euro struggles to find much traction and the Stoxx 600 is nursing a small loss. US index futures are firm. Aside from the Japanese government benchmark 10-year yield, which slipped a basis point, most other major bond yields are firmer. European yields are mostly 2-4 bp higher, with the increase in France leading the region and the 10-year Gilts doing best with its nearly flat performance. The 10-year Treasury yield is slightly firmer to a fraction above 4.0%. Canada's 10-year yield is about 3.5 bp higher at 3.09%. Gold is off 1.6% after rising almost 0.70% yesterday. Today's low is slightly below $4048. It settled around $4356 on Monday. December WTI is in a narrow range (~$61.20-$62.15) at the upper end of yesterday's surge that lifted it to about $62.20.

USD: The Dollar Index reached almost 99.15 in the middle of the week, and it held yesterday. It remains firm today but in about a 10-tick range on either side of 99.00. Last week's high was almost 99.50. DXY consolidated yesterday and also held above Wednesday's low, a little below 98.80. Today is the busiest the US economic calendar has been since the government shut down a little more than three weeks ago. In order to allow cost-of-living adjustments in Social Security and other programs, the September CPI will be reported today. A 0.4% rise in the headline will lift the year-over-year rate for the fifth consecutive month, and at 3.1%, it would be the highest since May 2024. The core rate has also risen for the past four months, but the median forecast in Bloomberg's survey sees it flat last month at 3.1%. Most Fed officials want to look through the uptick in inflation, with many seeing the impact of the tariffs as being temporary. Moreover, the balance of risks suggests the threat of further deterioration of the labor market is greater. The market has a Fed cut nearly fully discounted for next week, and given the blackout period until the meeting, there is little that officials can say to frame it differently. The preliminary October PMI is also due today, and it is expected to be softer. The composite PMI may have fallen for the third consecutive month and the fourth time in five months. Barring a dramatic revision, the final University of Michigan consumer survey is unlikely to have much impact, and the same can be said of Kansas City Fed's service survey.

Euro: The euro recorded an inside day yesterday, and the market seems to lack near-term conviction. With the Fed and ECB meeting next week, the Trump-Xi meeting at the end of the week, the escalation of sanctions on Russia, uncertainty is running high. Even the stronger-than-expected PMI failed to have much impact, and the euro is in a $1.1600-30 range. The preliminary manufacturing PMI rose to 50.0 from 49.8. The services PMI rose to 52.6 from 51.3. It is the highest since August 2024. The composite PMI rose to 52.2 from 51.2. It is a new high for the year, matching last year's high, which is the best since May 2023. Through September, it averaged 50.6 this year compared with 50.3 in the Jan-Sept 2024 period. Stronger German services appear to explain the strength, which lifted its composite to its best level since May 2023 (53.8 vs. 52.0). France's composite PMI fell to 46.8 (from 48.1). It is the lowest since February's 45.1 reading.

CNY: The PBOC has pushed the dollar fix to new lows. It was set at CNY7.0918 yesterday, the lowest since last October. It was set at CNY7.0928 today. A week ago, it was almost CNY7.0950. Nevertheless, against the offshore yuan, the dollar recorded the low so far for the year near CNH7.0850 on September 17, when the Federal Reserve cut the rates. For the third consecutive session, the dollar is in a CNH7.1230-CNH7.1300 range. Trump and Xi are still likely to meet on the sidelines of the APEC meeting at the end of next week. While Trump holds out the possibility of a deal, the latest sanctions on Russia are also an indirect way to discourage Chinese companies buying Russian oil due to the threat of secondary sanctions. The US is also threatening to impose broad software curbs on China, and it is not coincidental that China's recent press release on the broader and tighter critical mineral export controls was not using Microsoft software.

JPY: The jump in oil prices, the rise in US yields, and a growing conviction that the Bank of Japan will stand pat next week took a toll on the yen. The dollar rose to JPY152.80 yesterday and traded a little above JPY153.00 today. The greenback reached an eight-month high slightly above JPY153.25 on October 10. The US 10-year yield settled fractionally above 4.0% yesterday, and the little more than five basis point increase was the largest since September 17, when the Federal Reserve cut rates. This may have also supported the greenback. Japan reported the November CPI earlier today. As hinted by the Tokyo report out late last month, the headline and core rates rose to 2.9% from 2.7%, while the measure that excludes fresh food and energy eased to 3.0% from 3.3% and is the lowest print since April. Separately, the preliminary October PMI showed the manufacturing contraction continued (48.3 vs. 48.5), while the service PMI continued to expand but slower (52.4 vs. 53.3). The composite PMI slipped to 50.9 from 51.3. The 51.6 average in Q3 was the highest quarterly average since Q3 24. A week ago, the swaps market had a little more than a 25% chance of a BOJ hike next week discounted. It has been more than halved this week.

GBP: Sterling recorded an inside day yesterday and slipped a few hundredths of a cent below Wednesday's low (~$1.3305) but held above $1.33. It has not been above $1.3335 today. Unless it recovers further today, it will be the fifth consecutive session of lower highs. The swaps market is discounting a year-end base rate of about 3.80%, which is off 6-7 basis points this week to around a two-month low. The current target is 4.0%. Today's data points were stronger than expected, and still sterling struggles. The UK reported a 0.5% increase in September retail sales. The median forecast in Bloomberg's survey anticipated a 0.4% pullback after a 0.5% gain in August (which was revised to 0.6%). Also, earlier today, the preliminary October PMI posted better manufacturing (49.6 vs 46.2) and services (51.1 vs. 50.8). The composite rose to 51.1, a three-month high, from 50.1. It averaged 50.9 in the first three quarters of the year.

CAD: After rising to a six-month high last week, near CAD1.4080, the US dollar pulled back in recent days. Wednesday's low was near CAD1.3975, and yesterday, the dollar held CAD1.3980. Still, for the second consecutive session, the greenback settled below CAD1.40. However, the US dollar is posting a big outside up day against the loonie. The greenback first slipped through yesterday's lows, but the furious US suspended all trade negotiations with Canada over the Ontario province ad, ran using excerpts of a 1987 speech by then President Reagan, who defended the principle of free trade and was critical of tariffs. The clip of Reagan has been on social media for months. In fairness, the former president's foundation and institute were critical of Ontario's ad, claiming that its permission was not sought and that the "selective" use misrepresents Reagan's "full address". The US dollar recovered and reached almost CAD1.4030, the (61.8%) retracement of this week's losses. The week's high was set Tuesday near CAD1.4065. The Bank of Canada meets next week. The swaps market has about a 72% chance of a cut discounted, which is little changed from a week ago when the swaps market put the odds of a cut slightly below 70%. In Bloomberg's survey, 10 of 14 economists (~70%) expect a cut.

AUD: For almost two weeks, the Australian dollar has been chopping roughly between $0.6440 and $0.6535. It has been capped in front of $0.6520 today and is probing the $0.6490 area in late European morning dealings. A key question is whether it is a base or prelude to another leg down. Given the overextended momentum indicators, which have begun turning up, we suspect it is the former. Australia's preliminary October manufacturing PMI edged unexpectedly down to 49.7 from 51.4 in September. It was the second consecutive pullback and the first sub-50 reading of the year. The services PMI firmed to 53.1 from 52.4. It ended last year at 50.8. The composite PMI rose to 52.6 from 52.4. It averaged 52.0 in the first nine months of the year compared with 51.3 in the Jan-Sept 2024 period. It was at 50.2 at the end of last year and has not been below 50.5 this year.

MXN: The dollar is coiling against the Mexican peso. An eight-day trendline comes in near MXN18.4350 today, and a slightly longer trendline off the lows is near MXN18.3770. Given the positioning of the momentum indicators, we are inclined to see a downside breakout. It is in a narrow range of about MXN18.3870-MXN18.4185 so far today. The greenback can ease back into the MXN18.25-30 area without ending the long-term consolidation, which began at the year's low set near MXN18.20 on September 17, when the Federal Reserve cut rates. Mexico reported firm August retail sales yesterday (0.6%, which is twice the year's average monthly gain) and slightly softer-than-expected inflation for the first half of October. An immediate risk for the Mexican peso is Argentina's legislative election on Sunday. There are arguably two key metrics of the outcome. First is the sheer number of votes cast for Milei's Liberty Advances party (LLA). If it is more than the Homeland Force party, then investors may conclude that Milei can still win re-election (2027). Second is that the LLA needs to secure at least a third of the seats in either chamber to ensure that presidential decrees cannot be blocked. Only 127 seats of 257 in the lower chamber are up for election, so a simple majority is out of reach. In the upper chamber, 24 of 72 seats are being contested. There seems to be a universal opinion, except, perhaps, in parts of the US Treasury, that regardless of the election, the peso will depreciate. The electoral may shape the speed and the extent of the move.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: BP: Why Reducing Green Ambitions And Refocusing On Oil Could Pay Off
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

BP (NYSE:BP) is a British oil and gas giant that's been going through a series of changes lately, scaling down its green ambitions to focus once again on oil and gas. With renewed focus on developing in oil and gas and significant asset sales announced, the company's new management team has to navigate a very unpredictable environment with falling oil prices, so let's see why I believe this might turn out to be an opportunity.

Despite the lower prices, the company's CAPEX reduction led to it making a similar adjusted FCF YoY in the first half of 2025. Last year, they made $10.25 billion in adjusted FCF, which is great for their $85.19 billion market cap. Note that they adjust this for the significant irregular changes in working capital. Without it, they would've made about $2.28 in the first half of 2025, which is not nearly as good. This includes things like the $1.1 billion Gulf of Mexico (Deepwater Horizon) settlement payments, which will continue through 2033.

With a new management team and possibly pressure from activist investor Elliott Management, BP is now trying to turn the business around, steering away from its previous focus on green energy (where they were investing massive amounts of money that rivaled the industry's giants) and getting back into their main area of expertise (oil and gas), being at a point where they're now selling various assets. Recently, the company's new chair pledged more asset sales and named its portfolio "overly complex," saying that they need to be faster in shifting their focus back to oil and gas.

This comes as BP has previously set a target to divest about $20 billion worth of assets, in an effort to lower its net debt from $26 billion to $14 billion to $18 billion by the end of 2027, while also aiming to focus on costs and efficiency.

Meanwhile, they're also advancing on other fronts, recently approving a $5 billion offshore drilling project in the Gulf of Mexico (Tiber-Guadalupe), approving its second new production platform with a production capacity of 80,000 barrels per day. This project is expected to begin production in 2030, so there's a long way to go until it would positively impact BP. This is part of the company's plans to develop their upstream with projects almost everywhere on the planet.

Financially, based on BP's latest report, we see a good position overall, with the current assets covering the current liabilities and a ton of assets to offset their debt. Still, the debt is also quite significant, standing at ~$54.5 billion if we look at the long-term finance debt alone, so focusing on it may not be that bad.

BP's next Earnings Report is scheduled on November 4, with EPS expected to fall to $0.77 on $44.64 billion in revenue. I believe this report will be quite important, as it can offer a wake up call for the market if the company is hit by the current environment and gets forced into some cuts/reductions/etc., potentially affecting their long-term growth plans. Regardless, they will offer important insights into international oil and gas, so it's definitely worth looking into, and they recently said that they see higher Q3 upstream production but also weak oil trading performance.

Dividends & Buybacks

As part of their long-term targets including the net debt one, they see a disciplined CAPEX spending of around $13 billion to $15 billion in 2026 and 2027 after ~$14.5 billion in 2025 (vs $16.2 billion in 2024). This would allow them to distribute 30% to 40% of the operating cash flow (note: operating, not FCF), which can be a solid yield throughout the cycle, with a base dividend that was increased by 4% now and expected to keep increasing it by at least 4%.

Currently, the dividend costs them about $5.4 billion (including $400 million for non-controlling interests), for a yield of ~5.85% on the ordinary shares. They also announced plans to execute $750 million in buybacks in Q3 after spending $1.1 billion in Q2, $1.8 billion in Q1 - part of their target of distributing excess cash through buybacks over time.

Still, if the buybacks continue to fall like this, that means less demand for the stock, potentially leading to lower prices especially if they announce some issues/cuts/etc. as a result of the environment.

Risks & Opportunities

As the company depends significantly on oil prices, the commodity falling due to OPEC's eliminated self-imposed production limits, plus all the global uncertainty should affect them in the near-term. Just because the stock didn't fall immediately, it doesn't mean a couple of bad earnings reports won't fuel a crash, so be aware that there can be a lot more volatility than what we've seen, especially since a lot has been going on, and plenty more things are yet to unfold, as mentioned below.

The recent rate cuts and especially the upcoming ones could lead to a revitalized demand in the face of a weakening production, which can be excellent for oil and gas. I generally mention rate cuts (US or international - both relevant in this case) for most other companies as their main near-term catalyst, as they could boost the demand for oil, gas and energy since they'd revitalize the economy, which is ultimately the reason why the Fed uses them and doing the opposite of rate hikes.

Combined with potential for less tariff uncertainty, a weaker dollar and OPEC reverting to previous production levels can be some of the next catalysts for higher oil and gas prices in the near future.

Why I mention US rate cuts for a company like BP is because the US plays a massive role as the leader in oil production and consumption. As you can see, the company is actually the largest consumer and producer (followed by China), significantly above India, Saudi Arabia or Russia. And, something related to that which can continue to accentuate the fall in oil prices are US tariffs and the rising risk of a trade war with China. These two are pivotal for the industry, so any disruption at a time like today can continue to pressure oil prices, which should have effects on all oil producers.

Still, as I said before, some things may seem ready to improve, but until that happens, let's not forget that gold has recently breached $4,300 per ounce and nearly got to $4,400 a few days ago before finally falling a bit. In general, times that are good for gold and other precious metals (probably an all-time record year now) may not be that good for oil. All the issues with OPEC, US debt, government shutdowns, political uncertainty, tariffs, trade war, etc. ultimately play a key role in the price of these commodities.

At the same time, oil falling can lead to them ultimately selling their assets at lower prices compared to what they would've gotten a couple of years ago. If oil prices are down, the potential buyers would also be hurt, potentially meaning that there's less money in the market to buy (probably) even more opportunities, forcing BP to reduce their expectations.

But, a potential catalyst for the general oil market can come from China's policy changes announced last year, with the first easing since 2010, with potential to see that translate into more demand for oil in China, especially in petrochemicals. As I said before, these petrochemicals (e.g. propylene and ethylene) could represent the country's "next stage" of oil demand growth, being used for various plastics and synthetic fibres, which we know the country then sells globally - which also means that global rate cuts could be great for China since they'd boost global trade and mean more money for Chinese companies. This goes hand in hand with their "Made in China" policies for production on top of the quality over quantity improvements. To give you an idea, I'll include this paragraph written by Ciarán Healy for the IEA again:

The speed and scale of the expansion of China's petrochemical sector dwarfs any historical precedent, roughly doubling the pace of earlier capacity additions in the Middle East and United States. Between 2019 and 2024, China is set to add as much production capacity for ethylene and propylene - the two most important petrochemical building blocks - as presently exists in Europe, Japan and Korea combined.

But, if all this restructuring and now divesting works, BP can have significant capital at a time when the market may be full of opportunities, which in itself can be enough to fuel a solid cycle of growth. However, this also means that they are depending significantly on their new management's decisions, and just because they're newer, nothing guarantees that they won't get bad deals on their assets and reinvest them into worse ones, potentially doing significant long-term damage to the underlying business.

Peer Comparison

Compared to peers like Shell (SHEL), Equinor (EQNR), TotalEnergies (TTE), Exxon Mobil (XOM), Chevron (CVX) or Occidental Petroleum (OXY), we see BP trading at some of the lowest valuation metrics despite BP being one of the best performing stocks over the past year. This could be even more interesting to see as the company's turnaround develops, potentially unlocking a good amount of value that's perhaps not visible now.

Valuation

With $10.25 billion in adjusted FCF last year, let's assume a conservative FCF of $7 billion in 2025 to account for weaker oil prices and to be extra conservative despite the lower CAPEX. Assuming that their turnaround works and they deliver accretion thanks to debt repayments and investments, let's assume a 4.5% CAGR over the first 5 years, followed by 3% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years, as well as a $1 billion in 2033 and an additional $300 million from 2034 as a result of the scheduled Deepwater Horizon payments being over, with a 2% terminal growth rate. I'll assume a WACC of 7% (above my estimated 5.8% based on a cost of equity of 7.15% and 4.2% cost of debt) for a better margin of safety, as well as $26 billion in net debt and 15.6 billion shares outstanding (equivalent of 2.6 billion ADS for a 6:1 conversion) based on their Q2 report. This gets us to an enterprise value of $173.17 billion and an equity value of $147.17 billion, meaning a price per ADS of $56.58, which is well above the current level.

Conclusions

Overall, I rate BP a Buy given their turnaround potential, backed by solid assets, strong cash flows and good shareholder returns potentially leading to a very good return. Risks remain, as the company's turnaround will depend significantly on their management's ability to take the right decisions and navigate this very unpredictable environment, and I'm actually looking forward to lower stock prices in the near-term to make the case for buying even better.

data.title: September CPI: Quenches Recent Data Drought, Inflation Rangebound
data.description:
No government. No problem. That has been the underlying theme over the past month as equity markets have largely shrugged off the continuing government shutdown. Today’s CPI release, coming on a quiet Friday morning, could have been an afterthought had it not been one of the first major economic releases since the start of the shutdown.

Ahead of the release, equity markets have been buoyed by strong batches of corporate earnings, especially in recent days, where names such as Intel (INTC), Coca-Cola (KO), and 3M (MMM), to name a few, have topped expectations. The earnings season has contributed to the S&P 500 (NYSEARCA:SPY) being up by nearly 1% over the course of the shutdown.

Markets were relatively quiet in the pre-market hours prior to the release, with all three indexes, including the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJI) and Nasdaq Composite (NDX), trading within 150 points. This came even on news that President Trump was terminating trade negotiations with Canada. The bond markets were also in check, with the 10-YR (US10Y) hovering in the 4% range.

Following today’s CPI release, I believe the Fed will remain on track to reduce rates by another quarter percentage point at their upcoming Federal Open Market Committee (“FOMC”) meeting next week due to the range-bound nature of the data. Here’s all that you need to know about the September CPI report.

Where Did Prices Increase in September?

The Labor Department reported Friday that headline inflation rose 3.0% YOY. While this represents a notable uptick to the 3% mark, following a reading of 2.9% in August, the increase came in lower than the 3.1% YOY increase that was expected by economists ahead of the release.

Contributing to the uptick was what I find a surprising increase in gasoline prices. During the month, the index rose 4.1%, up from the 1.9% reported in August. While the increase is a notable standout, CPI observers shouldn't be concerned, in my view, due to what is likely to be a pivot downwards in subsequent months.

Readings from the AAA, for example, have shown gasoline prices declining in recent weeks due to lower travel demand. In its most recent reporting, the AAA has shown the national average gas price nearly at $3.00/gallon, a milestone low mark.

The food index also continued to increase in September, up 0.2% on the month and 3.1% YOY. This was due mostly to the food-at-home index, which rose 0.3%, driven primarily by increases in four of the six major grocery food group indexes, including cereals and bakery products. This was accompanied by a 0.1% increase in the food away from home category.

What Core Categories Increased Or Decreased In September?

Core inflation rose slightly less than expected, with the index rising 0.2% on the month and 3% YOY. This represents an improvement from the pace seen previously.

Driving the favorable reading was a welcome decline in prices for used vehicles and medical care commodities, which declined 0.4% and 0.1%, respectively, during the month.

The declines were offset by continued stickiness elsewhere, including in categories such as shelter and insurance, as well as apparel, which rose 0.7% on the month.

Fed Policy Implications

In my view, September’s CPI data isn’t likely to move the needle much for the Fed ahead of next week’s FOMC meeting. Observers are projecting a quarter-point rate cut, with the CME FedWatch Tool showing a near certain chance that the target rate will be lowered to a range of 3.75%–4.00%. Today’s report is unlikely to change this story.

Despite the near-certain rate outlook, it’s worth noting the split inside the Fed over how quickly to ease. For example, Fed Governor Christopher Waller has recently voiced his opinions towards a more cautious 25bps cut. In comments, he noted that the Fed shouldn’t “rekindle inflationary pressures” by moving too fast.

On the flip side, counterpart Governor Stephen Miran has pushed for a larger 50bps reduction, pointing to rising geopolitical tensions and tariff effects as reasons to move more decisively.

With the government shutdown limiting the availability of key data, policymakers are in one sense flying blind and are relying more heavily on forecasts than on fresh indicators. But for now, this hasn’t posed an issue, and in my view, the base case remains intact.

Final Thoughts On September CPI Report

It’s been a long minute since investors have had a chance to digest a major economic release. That clearly hasn’t served as a deterrent to the upward momentum stemming from a strong quarterly earnings season. Today’s release is unlikely to derail the momentum.

In my view, September’s CPI release showed that inflation remains tolerably range-bound and unlikely to meaningfully alter the Fed’s course ahead of next week’s meeting. Headline inflation landed slightly below expectations, albeit higher than last month’s reading, and the increase is not a concern for me.

Most inflation categories remain tame. Higher gasoline prices were a primary factor in September inflation, but with prices on the decline, as shown in recent reports from the AAA, this should reverse in later readings.

With the data landing within expectations, I don’t see the narrative changing in the next week. The Fed is still on track to deliver another 25bps rate cut, with the CME FedWatch Tool showing near-unanimous expectations for such a move.

All considered, September’s CPI served to quench a recent economic data drought but little else, with equity markets and the Fed likely to continue forward in their current trajectories.

data.title: Moody's Corporation: Staying Positive On The Earnings Growth Momentum
data.description:
Investment action

I had a buy rating for Moody’s Corporation (NYSE:MCO) previously, as I thought the upside was still attractive in the next two years if it is able to meet consensus FY28 adj. EPS estimates. MCO’s Q3 2025 results instilled more confidence in me that it can continue to grow earnings at a nice trajectory. One that is supported by solid issuance activity, expanding exposure to private credit, and clear traction in GenAI monetization. I reiterate buy.

Q3 2025 Earnings Review

MCO Q3 2025 results were very strong. Revenue crossed the $2 billion mark for the first time, reaching $2.007 billion, up 11% y/y, with both segments contributing. Moody’s Investors Service [MIS] delivered a record quarter at $1.098 billion, growing 12% y/y, marking its third consecutive period above $1 billion. Moody’s Analytics [MA] also saw a solid performance, with revenue up 9% y/y to $909 million. Recurring revenue, which makes up 96% of MA, grew 11% y/y (8% organically in constant currency), while annual recurring revenue [ARR] reached ~$3.4 billion, up 8% organically. Consolidated adj. EBIT margin expanded 510 bps y/y to 52.9%, with both segments showing strong improvement. MIS margin expanded 560 bps to 65.2%, while MA margin expanded 400 bps to 34.3%. As a result, adj. EPS was up 22% y/y to $3.92. Management also upgraded their guide, now calling for FY 2025 revenue growth to be in the high-single-digit range, and adj. EPS guidance was raised to $14.50–$14.75, implying ~17% y/y growth at the midpoint.

Issuance Outlook

The health of the debt market is perhaps the most important factor for the MIS segment, and very encouragingly, things are working just nice. Per management numbers, expected refunding needs over the next four years are now more than $5 trillion; this is roughly double 2018 levels, representing a 10% CAGR from 2018 to 2025. Think about the size of this. The US GDP is $29 trillion (as of FY24).

Beyond the size, the mix itself looks increasingly favorable for MCO as well. Non-financial corporate refinancing in the US and EMEA grew 6% y/y, but speculative-grade debt grew even faster at 7% y/y, with US spec-grade bonds up 20% y/y and EMEA spec-grade bonds and loans each up ~20% y/y. This is great for MCO because an increased proportion of higher-yielding, speculative-grade debt carries higher fees, and that should drive up revenue growth. In the near term, management commentary also points to continued momentum from Q3 carrying into Q4, which sort of sets a bullish stage for 2026.

There are a few reasons to be as optimistic as management: credit spreads are near historic lows; there is strong investor demand; there is a Fed rate cut that could lower benchmark rates; and there are early signs of renewed M&A activity (per the Q3 earnings call). For the latter, the fact that MCO saw record quarterly revenue in the Rating Assessment Service [RAS] in Q3 is a bullish signal, as it serves as a leading indicator for M&A. Management is also hearing positive comments from bankers about M&A, so that is another positive data point to consider.

Lastly, there is also the AI data center build-out financing theme that could be huge. We know that there is strong demand for data centers, and there are big numbers thrown around the capex figure. All of these need financing, and there is only so much equity that one is willing to give up. Even SoftBank is seeking debt to invest in AI. Combining all these tailwinds, I am staying very bullish on the debt issuance outlook for the coming years.

Private Credit

In my July note, I discussed MCO’s growing presence in private credit as a smart move that meaningfully extends its growth runway. I was right here. Q3 2025 results provided more proof that MCO is gaining traction, with private credit-related deal volume up ~70% y/y and associated revenue growing >60% y/y. While this is from a small base, the trend is very positive.

Importantly, the growth is not concentrated in direct lending ratings (where MCOs continue to partner with MSCI on investor analytics), but in entirely new areas such as structured finance [SFG], securitization, financial institutions [FIG], and infrastructure finance [PPIF]. This matters for MCO investors that are interested in this private credit space because it means MCO is tapping on multiple parts of the private credit value chain. My view here is that this would prevent MCO from getting hurt when one area of the value chain “blows up”; and also, that this gives MCO an early “foothold” position to win demand when the private credit market matures over time.

GenAI

Gen AI is an area that I have spent more time on for MCO, and the Q3 results have dramatically improved my confidence in how MCO can benefit. All the metrics I have listed out previously were great, but it was the new $3 million-plus deal with a Tier 1 US bank that really convinced me that GenAI is going to be huge for MCO. In this deal, MCO deploys GenAI-enabled solutions to help the bank automate credit memo writing and implement early warning systems for real estate portfolios. This win is far more important than the ARR contributions and step-up in spending because it shows that MCO can package all its Gen AI tools and work with the customer’s data and workflow to address the needs of a large and complex organization. If MCO can capture this caliber of customer, it would have no problem addressing demand for the rest of the market, I believe.

Another development that is noteworthy is the expanded partnership with Salesforce (CRM). MCO will now incorporate its proprietary GenAI data and analytics within CRM’s Agentforce 360 platform. At the same time, MCO’s own agentic AI sales tool (which is built to automate certain workflows and deliver insights using MCO’s data) will be offered on CRM’s Agent Exchange. This is a deal that leans more favorably for MCO, in my view, as MCO is the one that gains exposure to a larger set of potential enterprise customer bases.

At this point, I think the bearish narrative that AI poses a structural threat to MCO should be dismissed. The idea that one can simply use GenAI and public data to create their own “database” to replicate MCO is just not applicable any longer. The value that MCO brings with its Gen AI far exceeds simple data compilation (besides, credit data is not always available anyway). The value is from MCO’s proprietary assets like Orbis and risk intelligence datasets and deploying those insights through agentic AI solutions that are directly embedded into customer workflows. For a large and complex organization (like the Tier-1 US bank), it makes sense for them to simply leverage MCO’s tools to reduce time-to-market. The faster they get this rolled out, the faster they can increase productivity to be 1 step ahead of peers. Reinventing the wheel using public data doesn’t just take a long time; a lot of effort (IT engineers, time, and cost) is needed, and at the end of the day, it might not work at all.

Valuation

Consensus has briefly adjusted their FY28 adj. EPS outlook up to $19.83 (from $19.32 previously), which I think makes sense given the better visibility into debt issuance, growing traction in private credit, and developments in MCO’s GenAI efforts. That said, multiples have trended down since July, which I think is unfair given the better earnings growth outlook. The conservative way to estimate the upside is by multiplying the current multiple (30x forward PE) on the FY28 adj. EPS estimate. This equates to ~$595, which is a decent upside from the current share price.

Risk

The new risks to highlight include renewed trade tensions or stress in private credit markets (which is hard to track). Both of which potentially hurt the solid issuance trends seen recently. In addition, successfully commercializing the Gen-AI initiatives may require a step up in reinvestments that may hurt near-term margins and EPS growth.

I would also reiterate that the US macro situation is not entirely out of the woods yet. If things go south, all the positive developments seen so far wouldn’t matter, as investors would start to price in poor debt issuance and lower earnings growth, to be conservative.

Conclusion

I reiterate a buy rating for MCO. There is visible momentum across both the MIS and MA segments, supported by positive issuance trends, traction in private credit, and the early commercial proof of MCO’s GenAI strategy. With consensus FY28 adj. EPS estimates now at $19.83, I still think the upside is attractive even if multiples were to stay compressed vs. July.

data.title: QQQI: The New Class Of The Nasdaq 100 High Income ETFs
data.description:
High yield derivative income ETFs have become all the rage over the past 18 months. Unfortunately, most of the attention is going towards the single stock (and, in some cases, leveraged single stock) covered call ETFs, which can sometimes offer annualized distribution yields of 100% or more.

But the old fashioned covered call ETFs that are based on a broad index and aim to deliver a high yield without going over the top are still pretty good too.

For years, the standard bearer for covered call strategies based on the Nasdaq 100 has been the Global X Nasdaq 100 Covered Call ETF (QYLD). Its strategy is sublimely simple - 1) own the Nasdaq 100, 2) write an at-the-money call option with a one-month expiration, 3) collect the premium, 4) repeat the process by selling a new call option in the following month.

Using this process, QYLD has done a terrific job of generating steady and predictable monthly income for shareholders. Over the past three years, monthly distributions have varied within a range of only about $0.17 to $0.20 per share, rising when volatility ticks up and vice versa.

Its strategy of distributing half of all option income premiums received up to a monthly cap of 1% has produced a very steady annualized distribution yield of around 12%.

In short, it's been a very durable high income generating machine!

But as is often the case with products that proven successful, competitors emerge. Today, there are about a half dozen ETFs that produce a similar strategy to QYLD based on the Nasdaq 100.

One competitor, however, has emerged from the pack - the NEOS Nasdaq 100 High Income ETF (NASDAQ:QQQI).

How Does QQQI Work?

QQQI is an actively-managed fund that seeks to generate high monthly income in a tax efficient manner while maintaining the potential for some share price appreciation.

It plans to distribute monthly income by investing in the constituents of the Nasdaq 100 while simultaneously implementing a data-driven call option strategy. This strategy can involve both selling and purchasing NDX index options in order to adjust the income/capital growth profile of the fund.

The key feature of any good actively-managed strategy is the ability to outperform the returns you might get from a standard index-benchmarked ETF. Just being actively-managed doesn't create opportunities in a vacuum. The fund's investment strategy needs to come with a differentiator that sets it up to produce superior risk-adjusted returns.

I feel that QQQI does that.

The "data-driven" part of the strategy is a bit of a black box that we won't have a full understanding of, but the quant-focused approach has so far done a pretty good job of positioning the fund correctly in order to capture optimal income/capital growth from the strategy.

This video from NEOS explains how QQQI operates.

Comparing QQQI to QYLD

If there's one drawback to QYLD, it's that it's inflexible. Tracking an index, the fund is unable to shift in response to market conditions. It's use of at-the-money call options may help maximize the yield the fund can generate, but it also caps most share price upside.

QQQI aims to address some of those issues. Its dynamic overlay strategy allows the managers the ability to add or reduce exposure depending on what the market is doing while maintaining that high yield. The use of out-of-the-money call options may potentially limit some income capture, but it also opens the door for capital growth. That's been an important feature over the past couple years as the Nasdaq 100 has been moving higher in nearly a straight line.

Here's a summary of how QYLD lines up with QQQI.

Global X NASDAQ 100 Covered Call ETF (QYLD) NEOS Nasdaq 100 High Income ETF (QQQI)
Issuer Global X NEOS
Launch Date December 11, 2013 January 30, 2024
Management Style Passive Active
Index CBOE Nasdaq-100 BuyWrite V2 Index none
Primary Strategy Holds Nasdaq 100 stocks and sells covered calls on the index Holds Nasdaq 100 stocks and both buys & sells covered calls on the index
Option Term One-month expiration One-month expiration
Option Moneyness At-the-money Out-of-the-money
Overlay Strategy 100% overlay at all times Dynamic
Objective High monthly income

High monthly income
Upside Potential Generally limited Some depending on credit spread
Expense Ratio 0.60% 0.68%
Assets Under Management (as of 10/20/25) $8.1 billion $5.8 billion
Distribution Frequency Monthly Monthly
Distribution Yield (ttm) (as of 9/30/25) ~13.6% ~14.3%
Tax Treatment Distributions often include return of capital; may be less tax-efficient Uses Section 1256 contracts (60% LT / 40% ST) for enhanced tax efficiency

A couple things to note:

QQQI doesn't explicitly post what its option overlay percentage is at any given point, so it's tougher to know where the fund is being positioned especially bullish or bearish.

We do, however, know that looking at the fund's latest holdings shows a short NDX call with a strike of 25,200 and another short NDX call with a strike of 25,600, both with expirations in late November. There's no long call listed, so we can probably assume that QQQI is positioned relatively neutrally right now.

Also, the tax treatment is worth mentioning. QQQI's use of Section 1256 contracts means that distributions have a more defined tax treatment that can prove advantageous in certain situations. Of QYLD's fiscal year distributions, more than 98% are characterized as return of capital. That can defer tax liabilities, but it also erodes the cost basis.

A lot of investors don't really consider the tax consequences of their investments. QYLD may or may not produce a better tax result based on your individual situation, but QQQI gives you more of a known quantity (and one structured to likely be better by comparison).

The yields are also interesting to note. QQQI has consistently produced a higher distribution yield than QYLD over its brief history. That means that shareholders are getting an income boost plus the potential for additional capital gains.

QQQI Performance Recap

QQQI and QYLD should never be expected to compete with QQQ in a rising market based on their structures. QQQI, however, has come pretty close on a total return basis.

QQQI likely layered some long call positions over the portfolio at least during parts of the AI boom. QYLD's returns have been steadier, but more in line with what we'd expect to see in a rising market - significantly lagging total returns.

QQQI may be more vulnerable during bear markets and corrections given its higher historical volatility. Over the long-term, however, that could play out in its favor given how stocks tend to rise more often than not.

Conclusion

For those seeking more of a pure income play, QYLD might be the better choice given its lower volatility profile, steadier returns and predictable income. There's certainly nothing wrong with that play if you're that kind of investor.

Overall, however, QQQI is my preference. Its growth tilt and active management have proven to be effective in positioning the fund in the right ways at the right time. The flexibility of adding to or reducing exposure is a key advantage if the management team can play it right. It's a short history, but it's worked so far.

If QQQI can maintain its distribution yield advantage too, it might prove to be the better choice for a wider group of income investors.

data.title: Ford Earnings: A Sigh Of Relief, But Not Enough To Justify A Buy Rating
data.description:
During the Q3 2025 earnings release of Ford Motor Company (NYSE:F) the main focus seems to have fallen on the impact of the fire at the aluminum supplier Novelis and the net tariff headwind.

These two developments have been over lingering on investors' minds in recent months, with the latter being responsible for the guidance suspension earlier this year.

The former is a more recent event and it did cause F share price to lag those of its peers in recent weeks. Ford is one of the biggest losers from the fire at the Novelis factory as it provides material for the production of the iconic F-150 truck.

Nonetheless, the market now seems relieved after Ford's management provided a revised outlook for FY 2025, alongside ranges for certain cost items that will continue to weigh on profitability into FY 2026 as well. Although the updated guidance (see the table below) is notably lower to the one provided in February of this year, the market was already anticipating that which is causing Ford share price to be up by low single digits following the release.

In addition to the $1bn net tariff impact in 2025, Ford would experience an EBIT headwind of $1.5bn to $2bn this year. However, the management expects to mitigate roughly $1bn in the following year.

Between 2025 and 2026, we expect Novelis to be a headwind of $1 billion or less. For 2025, we expect an adjusted EBIT headwind of $1.5 billion to $2 billion in the fourth quarter for Novelis, and we currently have line of sight to mitigate at least $1 billion in 2026, and we are working to improve the situation further.

Source: Ford Q3 2025 Earnings Transcript

All in all, the market was not caught off-guard by any of that new information and as a result F share price is not materially changed and continues to lag behind most of its peers, with the exception of Stellantis (STLA).

While tariffs and the fire make for very interesting and dramatic headlines, long-term investors do not need to pay much attention to these issues as they are already being priced-in and there's also significant uncertainty when forecasting the overall impact of trade tensions.

Operational Performance

When it comes to operational performance, there was nothing noteworthy during the third quarter of 2025 which further supports the somewhat muted share price reaction in the early hours. Moving beyond the more immediate impacts on Ford's business, however, the quarterly results confirmed some ongoing trends that I find unsettling.

As a starting point, Ford's management has once again stressed the fact that in-spite of the current tariff headwinds they should level the playing field within medium and heavy-duty trucks - the high margin segments for the company. According to the CEO, Ford has been put at a disadvantage by producing these vehicles in the United States and tariffs would now address the issue of more competitive imports.

The overall impact of this is still unclear and in the meantime Ford's automotive margin continues to decline.

On a segmented basis, there are two bright spots for the company - Ford Pro and Ford Credit, with the former bringing significantly higher profits. The good news is that lower spend in the government sub-segment of Ford Pro has been offset by strength in small to medium businesses. As a result, profits in Ford Pro segment reached $2bn in the third quarter.

Ford Blue - the internal combustion engine segment for retail customers, however, continues on the recent downtrend with operating profit continuously falling in each of the Q3 periods shown above. The EV segment was gradually moving towards breaking-even, but the most recent report has shown otherwise with losses now exceeding those in Q3 2023. On top of all that, Ford's management did not sound very optimistic as far as achieving profitability in the segment is concerned.

Year-to-date, Model e is at a $3.6 billion loss. Roughly $3 billion of this is from our first-generation products, Mach-E, Lightning, Puma, Explore and Capri. The balance is investment in our next-generation vehicles, including our UEB platform. The only practical way to improve the profitability of our Gen 1 vehicles is through one of the more of the following: pricing, new cost reductions and improved fixed cost leverage. Given current industry trends, it's clear scaling fixed costs is a challenge for most of the industry.

Source: Ford Q3 2025 Earnings Transcript

The last positive area of the business was Ford Credit, where improved financing margins contributed to yet another strong quarter. Profit improved significantly over the past 12 month when compared to FY 2024, but still remains below 2022.

The trend of rising auction values, however, seems to have come to an end during the last quarter and loss-to-receivables ratio has jumped to Q1 2025 highs. This is by no means a trend-setting event, but is something that investors should follow closely.

Conclusion

The Q3 2025 report of Ford gave investors a sigh of relief after all the uncertainty around one-off events the ongoing trade tensions. That is why, Ford share price might continue its ascend in the coming weeks and even months, provided there are no adverse events within the market. Having said that, however, the market was accurately pricing-in these recent headwinds and investors should not rely in the quarterly results as a reason to buy. Moreover, from operational point of view, the quarter presented more challenges that opportunities for investors.

data.title: Realty Income: My Top Pick For Income Investors (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Performance assessment

Realty Income Corporation (NYSE:O) has slightly lagged the broader market index since my last update on the stock:

I think my 'Market Perform/Hold' rating has been accurate.

Thesis

Now, I'm becoming more bullish on O. Here's why:

  1. Realty Income is set to expand its portfolio at attractive spreads
  2. The ingredients are in place to support higher investment activity as well
  3. Lower interest rates are a tailwind for the stock
  4. O stock is attractively valued at a discount to its comps and may be poised for a multiple re-rating
  5. The technicals are turning bullish
  6. Management's expectations reflect higher tenant credit risks

Realty Income is set to expand its portfolio at attractive spreads

Realty Income is finding ample opportunities to expand its portfolio in Europe this year:

The size of the opportunity in Europe is currently larger than that in the US with the added benefit of lower borrowing costs as well:

We again leaned into Europe which accounted for $889 million or 76% of our investment volume at a 7.3% weighted average initial cash yield. Europe remains a compelling growth market driven by a fragmented competitive landscape, a larger total addressable market than what is available in the United States and a cost of debt capital that is currently more favorable with euro borrowing costs approximately of 120 basis points inside of U.S. dollar debt costs for 10-year notes as of today.

- CEO Sumit Roy in the Q2 FY25 earnings call

Since that Q2 FY25 earnings call on 6 Aug'25, the Federal Reserve has has cut rates by 25bps, whilst the ECB has kept rates steady. Hence, I estimate that the EU cost of debt capital is still cheaper than the US, but by a smaller margin of close to 100bps rather than 120bps. The bottom line is that it is still very advantageous for Realty Income to expand in Europe. And my next point discusses why I expect this to continue:

The ingredients are in place to support higher investment activity as well

Realty Income is upgrading its investment guidance from $4 billion to $5 billion for FY25 along with their per-share AFFO guidance:

We deployed $1.2 billion into investments during the second quarter at an initial weighted average cash yield of 7.2%. These results were bolstered by our growing presence in Europe, which accounted for 76% of our investment volume. Supported by consistent first half operating results and a robust pipeline of opportunities, we're pleased to increase our 2025 investment guidance to approximately $5.0 billion and raise the low-end of our AFFO per share guidance to a range of $4.24 - $4.28.

- Q2 FY25 Press Release, Author's bolded highlight

This is a continuation of the higher opportunities they are seeing particularly in Europe. A lot of the incremental investment activity this year is expected to occur in H2 FY25, with the impact on AFFOs seen more in FY26:

The increase in acquisition volume by $1 billion, as you can tell, it's going to be back-end loaded, second half loaded. And so the impact that one would experience from -- and you correctly said these are all accretive transactions, we wouldn't be doing diluted transactions. The impact of which won't be experienced in 2025, but certainly we'll be -- we'll see the benefits of which in 2026.

- CEO Sumit Roy in the Q2 FY25 earnings call, Author's bolded highlight

Hence, I see a strong case for diluted AFFOs to increase a year from now by around 25%, which is the percentage amount of the incremental increase in investments from $4 billion to $5 billion:

Lower interest rates are a tailwind for the stock

In my last note on Realty Income, my expectations on a vibrant transactions landscape were muted due to views of a higher-for-longer rate environment.

In Q3 FY24, management had remarked that rate cuts can lead to a more attractive transaction landscape. However, it looks like a higher-for-longer rates environment is emerging as the probability of the Fed Funds rate remaining at 425-450bps 2 months from now has increased dramatically from ~50% to almost 86%...

- Hunting Alpha

Now, we are facing the exact opposite scenario. Looking ahead to mid-year next year, the market is pricing in a certainty of Fed rate cuts with a weighted average cut of 99bps from the current band of 400-425bps:

So this too supports the outlook for higher investment activity and portfolio growth going ahead.

O stock is attractively valued at a discount to its comps and may be poised for a multiple re-rating

Another tailwind we can expect from lower rates is P/AFFO multiple expansion from the current 14x levels back toward the long term averages of 17-18x:

And if you look at where we've traded traditionally, we've been right around 17 to 18x is the average multiple of our equity. Today, we trade at 14x. And a lot of this happened primarily because of this high negative correlation that we have with the 10-year [rate]. And so if the expectation is that the 10-year rate is going to start to come down, the expectation is that we will start to revert back to our average multiple, which could then allow us to do more without having to source more.

- CEO Sumit Roy in the 2025 BoFA Global Real Estate Conference, Author's bolded highlight

Currently, O stock's 1-yr fwd P/AFFO is at 14.14x vs. the sector median's 15.31x, corresponding to a 7.64% discount. So valuation is attractive from a relative perspective too.

The technicals are turning bullish

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

Relative Read of O vs SPX500

Relative to the S&P500, O is now in a range following an incumbent downtrend. I expect this ranging action to continue. In other words, I think we will see more sideways movement and hence performance in-line with the S&P500 going ahead.

Relative Read of O vs CBOE S&P Dividend Aristocrat Index

With a forward dividend yield of 5.33%, O is a member of the CBOE S&P500 Dividend Aristocrat Index (SPAI ticker in TradingView). When we compare against this index, my outlook is more bullish as there are higher lows being printed in the broader accumulation range and good bullish strength in the recent monthly candles.

Management's expectations reflect higher tenant credit risks

Management's commentary suggests increasing tenant credit risk in the overall portfolio. For example, the company's 2025 guidance for rent loss came in higher than historical expectations:

Our 2025 outlook contemplates approximately 75 basis points of potential rent loss, which is slightly higher than our historical experience but consistent with our expectations going into the year. Much of this credit loss is the result of certain tenants acquired through public M&A transactions we have consummated in recent years.

- CEO Sumit Roy in the Q2 FY25 earnings call

Currently, almost 5% of the overall credit portfolio is in the company’s internal watch list that monitors for potential financial stress. Now, the good news is 114 clients make up this cumulative watch list exposure, indicating good diversification of the credit risk. Still, we should take seriously management's caution that harder times may be coming.

...our credit watch list is at 4.6%. But what is very interesting about that credit watch list is we have more than 100 clients who represent that. So on average, each client is circa 4 basis points. So we have diversified that risk as well across a multitude of clients... So yes, well positioned, but I think hard times may come.

- CEO Sumit Roy in the 2025 BoFA Global Real Estate Conference

As for the cause of this increased risk with tenants' creditworthiness, In the Q2 FY25 earnings call, management attributed the elevated credit risk largely to the impact of tariffs stressing some tenant businesses. In terms of financial impact, whilst I do not expect any major write-offs just yet, I would not be surprised if the provisions for rent impairment mix increases in subsequent quarters:

Takeaway & Positioning

I think Realty Income looks good now, especially for yield-focused investors. This year, company is taking advantage of cheaper debt funding rates to capitalize on the higher number of opportunities present in Europe. And encouragingly, all indications are that this trend will continue, supported by a lower interest rate outlook for next year too. Moreover, a dovish interest rate environment also provides a catalyst for a re-rating in the stock, which is already trading at a discount vs sectoral median 1-yr fwd P/AFFOs multiples. The technicals charts also look good especially on O vs the Dividend Aristocrat Index.

A key risk monitorable to the bullish view are credit risks in the overall tenants portfolio. But I do not expect any major rent write-offs at this stage; just watch the provisions for impairment numbers every quarter.

Rating: 'Outperform/Buy'

How to interpret Hunting Alpha's ratings:

Strong Outperform/Strong Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence. I also have a net long position in the security in my personal portfolio.

Outperform/Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis

Market Perform/Hold: Expect the company to perform in-line with the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis

Underperform/Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis

Strong Underperform/Strong Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence

The typical time-horizon for my views is multiple quarters to more than a year. It is not set in stone. However, I will share updates on my changes in stance in a pinned comment to this article and may also publish a new article discussing the reasons for the change in view.
